header: Gerente de People & Culture — Maple Bear
hub_subtitle: Business English para People & Culture, comunicação executiva e a liderança da divisão do Canadá

lesson.menu_title: Influencing Without Authority -- Presenting Your People Strategy to Canadian Leadership
lesson.menu_desc: Inversão para ênfase, o vocabulário de estratégia de pessoas e a apresentação que o board adota -- 35 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 4 -- Influencing Without Authority
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Danielle Moreira | Aula 4 -- Influencing Without Authority

listenings.text: Danielle, it is Claire. Your slot with the board is on Thursday, and I want you to walk in knowing exactly what you are walking into. You have no authority in that room. Not one of those five people reports to you, and two of them have been running their region since before Maple Bear had a Canadian office. So the first thing to understand is that nothing gets adopted because you presented it. It gets adopted because somebody in that room decides to carry it. The second thing. They will not argue with your people strategy. They will argue with what it costs. Somebody will say that talent retention is a lovely problem to have in a year when we are cutting budgets, and if you answer that with values, you lose the room. Answer it with people analytics. Bring the number that says what one senior departure costs us, and put it next to the cost of the plan. And Danielle, do not soften it. Make a compelling case, and then stop talking. The silence after a strong argument is not awkward. It is the sound of five people deciding.

Danielle, a quick note before Thursday. I have watched a lot of people present to this board, and the ones who fail all fail in the same way. They present a plan. This board does not adopt plans. It adopts arguments. So, three things. Open with what the data shows, never with what you believe. The moment you say I feel, you become a consultant, and consultants get thanked and then ignored. Second, name the trade-off before somebody else names it for you. Every people strategy is a double-edged sword. Flexible work widens the talent pool and it weakens the informal culture, and if you pretend otherwise, one of the directors will say it out loud, and then it is his point and not yours. Third, when they push on cost, do not defend your budget. Give them the cost of doing nothing. Not only did our last two departures cost us eleven months of hiring, but they also took the succession plan with them. Say that sentence, and watch the room change.

paras: When Marina Ortiz joined the Halden Group as its first Director of People and Culture, she was given a title, a budget of almost nothing, and no authority whatsoever. The five regional directors did not report to her. They had built the company, they had survived two recessions, and they had never once been asked to adopt a people strategy written by somebody who had been in the building for six weeks. Her predecessor had presented a beautiful framework to the board, had watched it approved, and had watched nothing happen afterwards. The plan was never rejected. It was simply never carried by anyone.

Marina did something different. Before she proposed anything, she spent a quarter inside the numbers. The engagement survey had been running for four years, and nobody had ever read it region by region. When she did, the story stopped being about culture and started being about money. One region was losing its senior teachers at three times the rate of the others, every departure was costing the company eleven months of hiring, and the performance cycle in that region had not been completed since the pandemic. Talent retention, presented as a value, had been ignored for four years. Talent retention, presented as people analytics, took eleven minutes.

The board adopted her strategy in a single meeting, and it adopted it for a reason she had learned the hard way. Never had she seen a plan survive because it was right. Plans survive because somebody in the room decides to carry them. So she made a compelling case, and then she named the trade-off before anyone else could. Not only did the flexible model widen the talent pool, she told them, but it also weakened the informal culture that had held the company together for twenty years. It was a double-edged sword, and she intended to manage it rather than deny it. Hardly had she finished when the regional director who had quietly killed her predecessor's framework asked to own the first pilot.
reading.source: Case study adapted for Alumni -- Halden Group (fictional)

reading.prompt: Which sentence is the main idea of the case?
choices: A people strategy is adopted when the person who presents it has authority over the room.
A people strategy is adopted when it is argued with data and its trade-off is named before the room can name it.
The Halden Group had a toxic culture in one of its five regions.

items: Marina had formal authority over the five regional directors.
They did not report to her. She was given a title and no authority whatsoever.
The board rejected the framework her predecessor presented.
The board approved it. Nobody ever carried it, which is a different kind of failure.
Nobody had read the engagement survey region by region before Marina did.
It had been running for four years, and nobody had ever read it that way.
Marina named the weakness of the flexible model herself, before the board could.
Not only did it widen the talent pool, she said, but it also weakened the informal culture.
The director who had blocked the previous plan asked to own the first pilot.
Hardly had she finished when he asked for it.

items.situation: "Why should we invest in retention in a year when we are cutting budgets?"
tips: What the data shows us is that one senior departure costs us eleven months of hiring, and we have had four this year.
answer cost with cost -- never with values

items.situation: "Your engagement survey measures a mood. It is not a metric."
tips: I would like to draw your attention to the regional split: one region is losing senior staff at three times the rate of the others.
one number they cannot argue with

items.situation: "None of these directors report to you. Why would any of them do this?"
tips: Because I am not asking them to adopt my plan. I am asking one of them to own the first pilot.
influence without authority -- give the plan away and it gets carried

items.situation: "And what is the downside of this flexible model of yours?"
tips: It is a double-edged sword: not only did it widen our talent pool, but it also weakened the informal culture.
name the trade-off before the room names it for you

items.situation: "This is a great deal of change for a single year."
tips: The rationale behind this approach is that we phase out the old performance cycle over two quarters. We do not switch it off in one.

items.situation: "So what exactly are you asking this board for?"
tips: What this means in practice is one owner per pillar, one metric, and a review in ninety days.
close with the ask, never with the summary
